This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Romania_national_football_team "Special:EditPage/Romania national football team") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(November 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

([Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Echipa națională de fotbal a României*) represents [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") in men's international [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). It is controlled by the [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation") (FRF; *Federația Română de Fotbal*), the governing body responsible for [football in Romania](/wiki/Football_in_Romania "Football in Romania"). The team is commonly nicknamed the *Tricolorii* ("the Tricolours") and usually plays its home matches at the [Arena Națională](/wiki/Arena_Na%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Arena Națională") in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest").

The **Romania national football team** ([Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Echipa națională de fotbal a României*) represents [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") in men's international [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). It is controlled by the [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation") (FRF; *Federația Română de Fotbal*), the governing body responsible for [football in Romania](/wiki/Football_in_Romania "Football in Romania"). The team is commonly nicknamed the *Tricolorii* ("the Tricolours") and usually plays its home matches at the [Arena Națională](/wiki/Arena_Na%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Arena Națională") in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest").

Romania has appeared at seven editions of the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"), and was one of only four European nations—alongside [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team"), [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"), and [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia_national_football_team "Yugoslavia national football team")—to compete at the inaugural tournament in [1930](/wiki/1930_FIFA_World_Cup "1930 FIFA World Cup"). The team qualified for all pre-[World War II](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") editions of the competition, returned in [1970](/wiki/1970_FIFA_World_Cup "1970 FIFA World Cup"), and later produced a [golden generation](/wiki/Golden_generation "Golden generation") during the 1990s that reached three consecutive World Cups from [1990](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup") to [1998](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup"). Romania's best World Cup campaign came in [1994](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup"), when led by playmaker [Gheorghe Hagi](/wiki/Gheorghe_Hagi "Gheorghe Hagi") defeated [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team") 3–2 in the round of 16, before narrowly losing to [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team") on penalties in the quarter-finals.

The national team has also qualified for the [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") six times, making its debut in [1984](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1984 "UEFA Euro 1984") and most recently competing in [2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024 "UEFA Euro 2024"). Its best result came at [Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000"), where Romania advanced from a [group of death](/wiki/Group_of_death "Group of death") containing [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team"), and [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team"), before being eliminated by eventual runners-up [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") in the quarter-finals.

In regional competitions, Romania was the most successful side at the [Balkan Cup](/wiki/Balkan_Cup "Balkan Cup"), winning the tournament a record four times between [1929](/wiki/1929%E2%80%9331_Balkan_Cup "1929–31 Balkan Cup") and [1980](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9380_Balkan_Cup "1977–80 Balkan Cup").

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Rumania_v_Per%C3%BA%2C_Los_Sports%2C_1930-07-25_%28385%29_02.jpg/330px-Rumania_v_Per%C3%BA%2C_Los_Sports%2C_1930-07-25_%28385%29_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rumania_v_Per%C3%BA,_Los_Sports,_1930-07-25_(385)_02.jpg)

Romania playing against [Peru](/wiki/Peru_national_football_team "Peru national football team") at the [1930 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1930_FIFA_World_Cup "1930 FIFA World Cup") in Uruguay

The Romanian Football Federation (*Federația Română de Fotbal*) was established in October 1909 in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"). Romania played their first international match on 8 June 1922, a 2–1 win over [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia_national_football_team "Yugoslavia national football team") in [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade"), being coached by Teofil Moraru.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Several temporary coaches were employed, before Moraru resumed control in August 1924, managing the side for nearly four years. Romania enjoyed some success during the 1930s; manager [Costel Rădulescu](/wiki/Constantin_R%C4%83dulescu_(footballer,_born_1896) "Constantin Rădulescu (footballer, born 1896)") took them to the first three [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") tournaments, a feat matched only by [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team") and [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team").

#### World Cups in the 1930s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: World Cups in the 1930s")]

At the [1930 World Cup](/wiki/1930_FIFA_World_Cup "1930 FIFA World Cup"), Romania won their first match against [Peru](/wiki/Peru_national_football_team "Peru national football team"), 3–1, with goals from [Adalbert Deșu](/wiki/Adalbert_De%C8%99u "Adalbert Deșu"), [Constantin Stanciu](/wiki/Constantin_Stanciu "Constantin Stanciu"), and [Nicolae Kovács](/wiki/Nicolae_Kov%C3%A1cs "Nicolae Kovács") and [Samuel Zauber](/wiki/Samuel_Zauber "Samuel Zauber") as goalkeeper, before losing 4–0 by hosts and eventual winners [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_national_football_team "Uruguay national football team").

Romania qualified for the [next World Cup in 1934](/wiki/1934_FIFA_World_Cup "1934 FIFA World Cup") after beating Yugoslavia 2–1 in a repeat of their first international. At the finals, Romania played only one game in a new knock-out format, losing 2–1 to [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_national_football_team "Czechoslovakia national football team") in [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste"), Italy, with [Ștefan Dobay](/wiki/%C8%98tefan_Dobay "Ștefan Dobay") scoring their only goal of the tournament.

Romania qualified by default for the [1938 World Cup](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup "1938 FIFA World Cup") after their qualifying playoff opponents [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_national_football_team "Egypt national football team") withdrew. They suffered a shock defeat in the finals in France, losing to minnows [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba_national_football_team "Cuba national football team"), who, like Romania, had only qualified due to the withdrawal of their qualifying opponents, the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team"). The first match at the [Stade du T.O.E.C.](/wiki/Stade_Chapou "Stade Chapou") in [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse") ended 3–3 after extra time, but Cuba won the replay four days later 2–1.

#### 1970 World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1970 World Cup")]

Despite a 3–0 loss to [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team") in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") and two draws against [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team"), Romania was able to qualify for the [1970 World Cup](/wiki/1970_FIFA_World_Cup "1970 FIFA World Cup") in Mexico. [Angelo Niculescu](/wiki/Angelo_Niculescu "Angelo Niculescu")'s promising side were given the toughest of draws, in Group 3 with [1966](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup") winners [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team"), giants Brazil and Czechoslovakia.

A [Geoff Hurst](/wiki/Geoff_Hurst "Geoff Hurst") goal gave England a narrow victory in Romania's first match at the [Estadio Jalisco](/wiki/Estadio_Jalisco "Estadio Jalisco") in [Guadalajara](/wiki/Guadalajara,_Jalisco "Guadalajara, Jalisco") in what was a very physical game. Chances were improved with a 2–1 win over the Czechs. After going behind early to a [Ladislav Petráš](/wiki/Ladislav_Petr%C3%A1%C5%A1 "Ladislav Petráš") goal, Romania turned it around after half-time with [Alexandru Neagu](/wiki/Alexandru_Neagu "Alexandru Neagu") and [Florea Dumitrache](/wiki/Florea_Dumitrache "Florea Dumitrache") scoring to give them two vital points. Even then, only a win over the Brazilians would take them into the quarter-finals.

There were rumours before the match that Brazil might prefer Romania to progress than world champions England;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] after beating them 1–0 in their previous match in Guadalajara, the South American giants still viewed England as one of its biggest obstacles to tournament victory. But Brazil played some of the best football of the competition, with [Pelé](/wiki/Pel%C3%A9 "Pelé") scoring twice and a [Jairzinho](/wiki/Jairzinho "Jairzinho") goal in between. Romania battled bravely; Dumitrache pulled the score back to 2–1 before the break and a late [Emerich Dembrowski](/wiki/Emerich_Dembrowski "Emerich Dembrowski") goal made it 3–2, but they were out.

#### 1972 to 1978

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1972 to 1978")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Oefenwedstrijd_WK_voetbal%2C_Nederland_tegen_Roemeni%C3%AB_0-0_Piet_Keizer_in_actie%2C_Bestanddeelnr_927-2330.jpg/330px-Oefenwedstrijd_WK_voetbal%2C_Nederland_tegen_Roemeni%C3%AB_0-0_Piet_Keizer_in_actie%2C_Bestanddeelnr_927-2330.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oefenwedstrijd_WK_voetbal,_Nederland_tegen_Roemeni%C3%AB_0-0_Piet_Keizer_in_actie,_Bestanddeelnr_927-2330.jpg)

Romania and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") drawing goalless at [De Kuip](/wiki/De_Kuip "De Kuip"), 1974

On 26 September 1972, under new coach [Valentin Stanescu](/wiki/Valentin_Stanescu "Valentin Stanescu"), Romania suffered a significant defeat to [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany_national_football_team "East Germany national football team") in [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig"). The East Germans won 2–0 to effectively seal their first ever qualification for the World Cup, which [would be held over the border](/wiki/1974_FIFA_World_Cup "1974 FIFA World Cup") in West Germany. With East Germany scoring a predictable 4–1 win in [Albania](/wiki/Albania_national_football_team "Albania national football team"), Romania were out, despite a huge 9–0 win over [Finland](/wiki/Finland_national_football_team "Finland national football team") in Bucharest.

Romania continued to suffer poor form in the [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship"). In their qualifying group for the [1976 European Football Championship](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1976_qualifying#Group_4 "UEFA Euro 1976 qualifying"), they were out-qualified by [Spain](/wiki/Spain_men%27s_national_football_team "Spain men's national football team") despite an impressive 1–1 draw in the away match. Romania failed to win matches, drawing twice with [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") and Spain and dropping points in [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark_national_football_team "Denmark national football team") with a dismal goalless draw.

Romania were again beaten by Spain for a place in the [1978 World Cup](/wiki/1978_FIFA_World_Cup "1978 FIFA World Cup") in Argentina. After a 1–0 win in Bucharest, Romania lost a match at home to Yugoslavia 6–4 having led 3–2 at half time. Spain won 1–0 in Belgrade to seal passage to Argentina.

#### 1984 European Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1984 European Championship")]

Main article: [UEFA Euro 1984 qualifying Group 5](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1984_qualifying_Group_5 "UEFA Euro 1984 qualifying Group 5")

Romania's sole successful qualifying campaign between 1970 and 1990 was for the [European Championships in 1984](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1984 "UEFA Euro 1984") in France. At the finals, Romania were drawn with Spain, holders [West Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") and Portugal. Under head coach [Mircea Lucescu](/wiki/Mircea_Lucescu "Mircea Lucescu"), an encouraging opening game in [Saint-Étienne](/wiki/Saint-%C3%89tienne "Saint-Étienne") saw them draw with the Spanish. [Francisco José Carrasco](/wiki/Francisco_Jos%C3%A9_Carrasco "Francisco José Carrasco") opened the scoring from the penalty spot but Romania equalized before half-time with a goal from [Laszlo Bölöni](/wiki/Laszlo_B%C3%B6l%C3%B6ni "Laszlo Bölöni").

Against the Germans in [Lens](/wiki/Lens,_Pas-de-Calais "Lens, Pas-de-Calais"), [Marcel Coraș](/wiki/Marcel_Cora%C8%99 "Marcel Coraș") scored an equalizer in the first minute of the second half in response to [Rudi Völler](/wiki/Rudi_V%C3%B6ller "Rudi Völler")'s opener, but Völler would score a winning goal. Their last match in [Nantes](/wiki/Nantes "Nantes") was a must-win match, but [Nené](/wiki/Nen%C3%A9_(footballer,_born_1949) "Nené (footballer, born 1949)")'s late winner meant Portugal progressed with Spain, who netted a dramatic late winner against West Germany at the [Parc des Princes](/wiki/Parc_des_Princes "Parc des Princes") in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris").

Romania stuttered throughout the rest of the decade, but a stronger squad at the end of the decade saw them qualify for their fifth World Cup in [1990](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup"). A win over Denmark in their last match took [Emerich Jenei](/wiki/Emerich_Jenei "Emerich Jenei")'s side to the finals for the first time in 20 years.

#### 1990 World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1990 World Cup")]

See also: [1990 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 1](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_1 "1990 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 1")

With an increasing trend for big clubs in [Italy](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") and [Spain](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") buying up the best foreign talent[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], Romania's squad was entirely domestic-based. Midfielder [Ilie Dumitrescu](/wiki/Ilie_Dumitrescu "Ilie Dumitrescu"), [striker](/wiki/Striker_(football) "Striker (football)") [Florin Răducioiu](/wiki/Florin_R%C4%83ducioiu "Florin Răducioiu") and genius playmaker[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Gheorghe Hagi](/wiki/Gheorghe_Hagi "Gheorghe Hagi"), were in the squad. After world champions Argentina were stunned by [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon_national_football_team "Cameroon national football team") in the tournament's opening match, Romania did their chances no harm with a convincing win over the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_football_team "Soviet Union national football team") at the [San Nicola](/wiki/Stadio_San_Nicola "Stadio San Nicola") in [Bari](/wiki/Bari "Bari"), with [Marius Lăcătuș](/wiki/Marius_L%C4%83c%C4%83tu%C8%99 "Marius Lăcătuș") scoring in each half. The result was all the more impressive[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] given the absence of Hagi. There was controversy, however, as Lăcătus' second was a penalty given for a handball by [Vagiz Khidiatullin](/wiki/Vagiz_Khidiatullin "Vagiz Khidiatullin") that television replays clearly showed to be some way outside the penalty area[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

Romania lost to Cameroon next; cult hero[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Roger Milla](/wiki/Roger_Milla "Roger Milla"), aged 38, came on as a substitute for [Emmanuel Maboang Kessack](/wiki/Emmanuel_Maboang_Kessack "Emmanuel Maboang Kessack") and scored twice before [Gavril Balint](/wiki/Gavril_Balint "Gavril Balint") pulled one back. Romania needed a point in their last match against improving Argentina at the [San Paolo](/wiki/Stadio_San_Paolo "Stadio San Paolo") in [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"); [Pedro Monzón](/wiki/Pedro_Monz%C3%B3n "Pedro Monzón") gave Argentina the lead after an hour, but Balint quickly equalized and Romania held on to reach the second round[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

Against [Jack Charlton](/wiki/Jack_Charlton "Jack Charlton")'s [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland_national_football_team "Republic of Ireland national football team") side in [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), Romania did not have the quality to break down a defensive opposition. [Daniel Timofte](/wiki/Daniel_Timofte "Daniel Timofte") was the only player to miss in the [penalty shoot-out](/wiki/Penalty_shootout_(association_football) "Penalty shootout (association football)") – his kick saved by [Packie Bonner](/wiki/Packie_Bonner "Packie Bonner") – and Romania were out[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

#### 1994 World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1994 World Cup")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/ROM_1994_MiNr4992_pm_B002.jpg/250px-ROM_1994_MiNr4992_pm_B002.jpg)](/wiki/File:ROM_1994_MiNr4992_pm_B002.jpg)

[1994 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup") stamp issued by [Poșta Română](/wiki/Po%C8%99ta_Rom%C3%A2n%C4%83 "Poșta Română")

Romania missed out on [Euro 1992](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1992 "UEFA Euro 1992")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") qualified after Romania drew a must-win last match in [Sofia](/wiki/Sofia "Sofia") against [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_national_football_team "Bulgaria national football team"), with [Nasko Sirakov](/wiki/Nasko_Sirakov "Nasko Sirakov")'s equalizer sealing their fate[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

Romania was successful, however, in [reaching another World Cup](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)") in the [United States](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup") in 1994. After losing in Belgium and suffering a heavy 5–2 defeat in Czechoslovakia, Romania went into their last match at [Cardiff Arms Park](/wiki/Cardiff_Arms_Park "Cardiff Arms Park") with [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") needing a win to pip them to a place in the finals. Goals from Gheorghe Hagi and [Dean Saunders](/wiki/Dean_Saunders "Dean Saunders") meant the game was finely balanced, before Wales were awarded a penalty[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. [Paul Bodin](/wiki/Paul_Bodin "Paul Bodin") of [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town_F.C. "Swindon Town F.C.") stepped up but hit the woodwork and Romania went on to win 2–1, Florin Răducioiu's late goal proving unnecessary as Czechoslovakia dropped a point in Belgium and were eliminated.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

At the finals, Romania were one of the most entertaining teams in the early stages[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], with Gheorghe Hagi, Florin Răducioiu and Ilie Dumitrescu on form. Romania beat [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia_national_football_team "Colombia national football team") 3–1 at the [Pasadena](/wiki/Pasadena,_California "Pasadena, California") [Rose Bowl](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_(stadium) "Rose Bowl (stadium)") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") (all but one of Romania's games took place in [California](/wiki/California "California"), and they were awarded the advantage of playing most of their games in LA). Răducioiu opened the scoring before Hagi scored a spectacular second from wide on the left touchline[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. [Adolfo Valencia](/wiki/Adolfo_Valencia "Adolfo Valencia") pulled one back with a headed goal just before half-time, but Romania held on and Răducioiu sealed the win with a late third[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

In [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit")'s indoor [Pontiac Silverdome](/wiki/Pontiac_Silverdome "Pontiac Silverdome"), the temperature soared due to the greenhouse effect in the indoor arena[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team"), acclimatized after having already played the hosts there, outran Romania in the second half and turned a 1–1 half time score into a 4–1 win. Romania responded by beating the hosts 1–0 in Pasadena with an early [Dan Petrescu](/wiki/Dan_Petrescu "Dan Petrescu") goal[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

In the Round of 16 knockout stage they faced Argentina, minus [Diego Maradona](/wiki/Diego_Maradona "Diego Maradona") who had been thrown out of the tournament for a positive drug test, in Los Angeles[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. The suspended Răducioiu was hardly missed, as coach [Anghel Iordănescu](/wiki/Anghel_Iord%C4%83nescu "Anghel Iordănescu") pushed Dumitrescu forward to play as a striker[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and the player responded by scoring twice in the first 20 minutes, one a subtle left foot flick from a right-wing Hagi cross slotted between the Argentine defenders. In between, [Gabriel Batistuta](/wiki/Gabriel_Batistuta "Gabriel Batistuta") scored a penalty, but after half-time Romania netted a third on the counterattack, with Hagi beating goalkeeper [Luis Islas](/wiki/Luis_Islas "Luis Islas"). [Abel Balbo](/wiki/Abel_Balbo "Abel Balbo") pulled one back, but Romania held on for a shock win[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

Romania would suffer penalty heartbreak again in the quarter-final against [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team") in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"). With just 13 minutes to play, a tight match opened up as Sweden's [Tomas Brolin](/wiki/Tomas_Brolin "Tomas Brolin") scored from a clever free-kick move, the ball passed outside the Romanian wall by [Håkan Mild](/wiki/H%C3%A5kan_Mild "Håkan Mild") for Brolin to smash in[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. Iordănescu threw caution to the wind and the returning Răducioiu found a late equalizer, again from a free-kick move but this time down to a deflection and a failure of the Swedes to clear. In extra time Răducioiu scored again after a mistake by [Patrik Andersson](/wiki/Patrik_Andersson "Patrik Andersson"), but Sweden then scored their own late equalizer as giant striker [Kennet Andersson](/wiki/Kennet_Andersson "Kennet Andersson") climbed above goalkeeper [Florin Prunea](/wiki/Florin_Prunea "Florin Prunea") to head home a long ball. Prunea had come in after two matches to replace [Bogdan Stelea](/wiki/Bogdan_Stelea "Bogdan Stelea"), whose confidence was shattered by the 4–1 loss to the Swiss[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. In the shoot-out, Dan Petrescu and [Miodrag Belodedici](/wiki/Miodrag_Belodedici "Miodrag Belodedici") had their kicks saved by [Thomas Ravelli](/wiki/Thomas_Ravelli "Thomas Ravelli") and Sweden went through[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

#### Euro 1996

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Euro 1996")]

At [Euro 1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996"), held in England, Romania arrived as a highly thought-of and popular team but had a nightmare. Iordănescu's side were based in the northeast, with their first two games at [St James' Park](/wiki/St_James%27_Park "St James' Park") in [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne"). Against France, they lost to a [Christophe Dugarry](/wiki/Christophe_Dugarry "Christophe Dugarry") header reminiscent of Kennet Andersson's two years earlier, beating the goalkeeper to a lofted through ball. An early goal from Bulgaria striker [Hristo Stoichkov](/wiki/Hristo_Stoichkov "Hristo Stoichkov") at St James' Park put Romania on the back foot in Euro 1996, but [Dorinel Munteanu](/wiki/Dorinel_Munteanu "Dorinel Munteanu") appeared to have kept Romania in the match – and in the tournament – with a thunderbolt that hit the bar, bounced over the line, and back out. Referee [Peter Mikkelsen](/wiki/Peter_Mikkelsen_(referee) "Peter Mikkelsen (referee)") merely waved play on, however, and Romania went on to lose the game 1–0, a defeat which sent them out of the tournament.

#### 1998 World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1998 World Cup")]

Despite a poor performance at Euro 1996, Romania impressed in [qualifying](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA)#Group_8 "1998 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)"), finishing ten points clear of the Republic of Ireland and were [seeded](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup_seeding "1998 FIFA World Cup seeding") for the final tournament of the [1998 World Cup](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup").

[Adrian Ilie](/wiki/Adrian_Ilie "Adrian Ilie") scored the only goal with a fine chip in their first match against Colombia at [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon")'s [Stade Gerland](/wiki/Stade_Gerland "Stade Gerland"). In Toulouse, they met an England side starting with prodigal striker [Michael Owen](/wiki/Michael_Owen "Michael Owen") on the bench, with [Teddy Sheringham](/wiki/Teddy_Sheringham "Teddy Sheringham") preferred alongside [Alan Shearer](/wiki/Alan_Shearer "Alan Shearer"). A mistake by [Tony Adams](/wiki/Tony_Adams_(footballer) "Tony Adams (footballer)") was punished by [Viorel Moldovan](/wiki/Viorel_Moldovan "Viorel Moldovan"), who played for [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C."), before Owen came on to claim an equalizer. But Romania won with a wonderful late goal from Dan Petrescu, also playing in England with [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C."), fighting off his club teammate [Graeme le Saux](/wiki/Graeme_le_Saux "Graeme le Saux") and [nutmegging](/wiki/Nutmeg_(football) "Nutmeg (football)") goalkeeper [David Seaman](/wiki/David_Seaman "David Seaman").

The next match was against a Tunisia side eliminated after losing to both England and Colombia. With England–Colombia being the more decisive game, the [Stade de France](/wiki/Stade_de_France "Stade de France") in Paris was an 80,000-strong sell out and the crowd were nearly rewarded with a shock as [Skander Souayah](/wiki/Skander_Souayah "Skander Souayah") scored an early penalty to give the north Africans the lead. Romania needed a point to win the group and avoid Argentina in the round of 16, and got it when Moldovan volleyed a late equalizer. It did them little good, however, as in the round of 16 match at [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux") against Croatia, [Davor Šuker](/wiki/Davor_%C5%A0uker "Davor Šuker") scored a twice-taken penalty to eliminate Romania.

#### Euro 2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Euro 2000")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/ENG-ROU_2000-06-20.svg/250px-ENG-ROU_2000-06-20.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ENG-ROU_2000-06-20.svg)

Line-ups for Romania versus England at the [UEFA Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000")

Romania had a strong qualifying campaign, winning a tough [Group 7](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000_qualifying_Group_7 "UEFA Euro 2000 qualifying Group 7") with Portugal, [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_national_football_team "Slovakia national football team"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_national_football_team "Hungary national football team"), [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan_national_football_team "Azerbaijan national football team") and [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein_national_football_team "Liechtenstein national football team"). The Romanians impressed, never losing and winning seven times, including a big upset in [Porto](/wiki/Porto "Porto") after defeating Portugal thanks to a late goal scored by Dorinel Munteanu. In Bucharest, the score finished 1–1.

At [Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000"), held in Belgium and the Netherlands, Romania were drawn against 1996 champions Germany, semi-finalists England and Portugal. The chances for the Romanians to qualify through quarter-finals were seen as slim.

Romania, however, started brightly against the Germans in [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège"), with Viorel Moldovan scoring from close range. A long-range [Mehmet Scholl](/wiki/Mehmet_Scholl "Mehmet Scholl") equalizer meant they had to be content with a point and their position looked shaky after [Costinha](/wiki/Costinha "Costinha") headed a last minute winner for Portugal in their second match.

[Emerich Jenei](/wiki/Emerich_Jenei "Emerich Jenei"), back as coach, threw caution to the wind in the last match in [Charleroi](/wiki/Charleroi "Charleroi") against England, a must-win match for Romania. Defender [Cristian Chivu](/wiki/Cristian_Chivu "Cristian Chivu")'s cross went in off the post in the 22nd minute, but despite Romania dominating, England led at half-time through an Alan Shearer penalty and a late Michael Owen goal after he rounded goalkeeper Bogdan Stelea to score a tap-in, both in the last five minutes of the half. Romania attacked after the break and were quickly rewarded; Dorinel Munteanu punishing a poor punch from [Nigel Martyn](/wiki/Nigel_Martyn "Nigel Martyn"), a late replacement for injured goalkeeper David Seaman, to equalize three minutes after the restart. England cracked under the pressure. Unable to retain possession or pose an attacking threat, they fell deep and late on [Phil Neville](/wiki/Phil_Neville "Phil Neville"), playing out of position at left-back, conceded a penalty scored by [Ioan Ganea](/wiki/Ioan_Ganea "Ioan Ganea") in the 89th minute.

Romania's relief was tempered by tough opposition in the last eight, and Italy, who would end up seconds from being crowned European champions in an agonizing final, comfortably saw them off 2–0 in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). [Francesco Totti](/wiki/Francesco_Totti "Francesco Totti") and [Filippo Inzaghi](/wiki/Filippo_Inzaghi "Filippo Inzaghi") scoring towards the end of the first half. In the 35th minute, Gheorghe Hagi, in his final international tournament, hit the woodwork with goalkeeper [Francesco Toldo](/wiki/Francesco_Toldo "Francesco Toldo") stranded off his line and, after the break, was sent off for diving. Romania's tournament was over and Emerich Jenei left his job as coach again.

### 2000s – World Cup dry spell

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2000s – World Cup dry spell")]

Romania failed to qualify for the next three major tournaments. They drew [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_national_football_team "Slovenia national football team"), who had been surprise qualifiers for Euro 2000 in a playoff for a place in the [2002 World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup") in South Korea and Japan. A narrow 2–1 deficit – having led through a [Marius Niculae](/wiki/Marius_Niculae "Marius Niculae") goal – after the first leg in [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana") was not irretrievable. With fan hero Gheorghe Hagi now coaching the side, they were confident of getting the win they needed in Bucharest against the [Balkan](/wiki/Balkan "Balkan") upstarts, but Slovenia took the lead before the hour through [Mladen Rudonja](/wiki/Mladen_Rudonja "Mladen Rudonja"). Right wing-back [Cosmin Contra](/wiki/Cosmin_Contra "Cosmin Contra") quickly equalized but Romania could not find the goal they needed to force extra time and Slovenia, with maverick manager [Srečko Katanec](/wiki/Sre%C4%8Dko_Katanec "Srečko Katanec"), were in a major tournament again.

#### Euro 2004

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Euro 2004")]

Romania were confident of qualifying for the tournament, drawn in Group 2 with seeds Denmark, [Norway](/wiki/Norway_national_football_team "Norway national football team"), [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina_national_football_team "Bosnia and Herzegovina national football team") and minnows [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_national_football_team "Luxembourg national football team"), with Anghel Iordănescu back as coach. Despite a good start – a 3–0 win away to Bosnia in [Sarajevo](/wiki/Sarajevo "Sarajevo") – Romania stuttered. [Steffen Iversen](/wiki/Steffen_Iversen "Steffen Iversen")'s late goal gave Norway a surprise win in Bucharest and they were stunned at home by the Danes, 5–2, with [Thomas Gravesen](/wiki/Thomas_Gravesen "Thomas Gravesen") scoring a spectacular goal from around 50 yards out, after leading twice. They recovered slightly, completing a double over the Bosnians and earning a point in [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"), but conceded a cutting injury time equalizer in Denmark to draw 2–2. It was decisive, as they now required Norway to fail to win at home to Luxembourg to stand any realistic chance of qualifying. Eventually, the Danes got a point in Bosnia to scrape through a tight group, with Norway going to a play-off with Spain.

#### 2006 World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2006 World Cup")]

Romania were drawn with the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") and the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_national_football_team "Czech Republic national football team"), who were ranked first and second in Europe respectively in [2006 World Cup](/wiki/2006_World_Cup "2006 World Cup") [qualification](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2006 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)"). Early wins over Finland and [Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia_national_football_team "North Macedonia national football team") were unconvincing, and they were behind the two leaders by the time they earned a 2–0 home win over the Czechs. Despite a record of eight wins, three losses and one draw, they finished third behind the Dutch and the Czechs and missed out on another major tournament.

#### Euro 2008

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Euro 2008")]

Romania were drawn in a group with the Netherlands again, along with Bulgaria for [Euro 2008 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying"). Romania, however, performed well, losing only away against Bulgaria and beating the Netherlands 1–0 at home with a goal scored by [Dorin Goian](/wiki/Dorin_Goian "Dorin Goian") from a suspicious [offside position](/wiki/Offside_(association_football) "Offside (association football)") not seen by referee [Kyros Vassaras](/wiki/Kyros_Vassaras "Kyros Vassaras"). On 17 October 2007, Romania became the fourth team to qualify for [Euro 2008](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008 "UEFA Euro 2008"), the nation's first international tournament since Euro 2000. Coincidentally, [Victor Pițurcă](/wiki/Victor_Pi%C8%9Burc%C4%83 "Victor Pițurcă") also led Romania to qualification for Euro 2000, only to sit back and let Emerich Jenei coach the team in the final tournament; this time, however, he stayed in the role, the first time he coached a national team in the final stages of a tournament.

Romania was drawn in the so-called "[Group of death](/wiki/Group_of_death "Group of death")" alongside the Netherlands, world champions Italy and France, runners-up in the 2006 World Cup. Romania started with a 0–0 draw against a lacklustre France while Italy were soundly beaten by the Netherlands, 3–0. In their next match, against Italy, [Adrian Mutu](/wiki/Adrian_Mutu "Adrian Mutu") opened the scoring early in the second half. Their lead was a very short one, however, as Italy's [Christian Panucci](/wiki/Christian_Panucci "Christian Panucci") scored a minute later off of a corner kick. Nearing the end of the match, [Daniel Niculae](/wiki/Daniel_Niculae "Daniel Niculae") earned a penalty for his team, but goalkeeper [Gianluigi Buffon](/wiki/Gianluigi_Buffon "Gianluigi Buffon") saved the subsequent Mutu penalty, leaving Romania with two points and needing a win against the Netherlands, who defeated France 4–1 that same evening. The Netherlands beat Romania 2–0 in the final game of the group, which meant that Italy joined the Netherlands in the quarter-finals and Romania finished third, ahead of France.

#### 2010 World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2010 World Cup")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Stamps_of_Romania%2C_2011-75.jpg/250px-Stamps_of_Romania%2C_2011-75.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamps_of_Romania,_2011-75.jpg)

[Arena Națională](/wiki/Arena_Na%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Arena Națională"), opened in 2011, the national stadium of Romania, as seen on a Romanian stamp (2011)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Romanian_fans.jpg/250px-Romanian_fans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Romanian_fans.jpg)

Romanian fans at the new Arena Națională in June 2013

Romania were drawn into the UEFA qualifying round for the [2010 World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup") alongside France, [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia_national_football_team "Serbia national football team"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria_national_football_team "Austria national football team"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_national_football_team "Lithuania national football team") and the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands_national_football_team "Faroe Islands national football team"). Although Romania were seeded in the second pot, they eventually finished fifth, above only the Faroe Islands. Their campaign was a disaster that began with a 3–0 home loss to Lithuania and included a 5–0 trashing in Belgrade by Serbia. Furthermore, various problems were caused during the poor campaign, such as the retirement from international football of Cosmin Contra, Mirel Rădoi and Adrian Mutu (the latter would later be recalled after a year's absence). Coach Victor Pițurcă resigned and was replaced by [Răzvan Lucescu](/wiki/R%C4%83zvan_Lucescu "Răzvan Lucescu").

#### Euro 2012

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Euro 2012")]

In [Euro 2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2012 qualifying") qualifying, Romania was drawn into [Group D](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_qualifying_Group_D "UEFA Euro 2012 qualifying Group D") along with France, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Belarus, Albania and Luxembourg. Although the team initially seemed prepared to continue their awful form from their disastrous World Cup campaign, beginning with a 1–1 draw with Pot 5 members Albania and following up with a goalless draw with Belarus and a pair of losses to France and Bosnia and Herzegovina, the team was able to rebound somewhat and register their first two victories. The first was a win against Luxembourg and the second was an important win in the rematch against Bosnia and Herzegovina. Romania's last decent result came when they battled France to a goalless draw before ending the campaign the way it began – two disappointing draws with Albania and Belarus. They finished qualification in a distant third place and only one point ahead of Belarus.

#### 2014 World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 2014 World Cup")]

Romania were drawn into the [2014 FIFA World Cup qualifying round](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "2014 FIFA World Cup qualification") with the Netherlands, [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team"), Hungary, [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia_national_football_team "Estonia national football team") and Andorra. They made an impressive start with a 2–0 away win in Estonia followed by a 4–0 win at home against modest Andorra and another away win in Turkey (1–0). After that, Romania were defeated by the Netherlands, both at home and away, and managed to secure only a draw in Hungary, in between. Romania started the last part of the campaign with a victory at home, against Hungary, but was defeated by Turkey. The last two match days were decisive, with Romania securing its place in the play-off with two wins, against Andorra and Estonia, while qualification rivals Turkey and Hungary were both defeated by the winner of the group, the Netherlands. Romania were drawn to play Greece for a place in the World Cup finals, but a 3–1 loss in Greece and a 1–1 home draw ended its run.

#### UEFA Euro 2016

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: UEFA Euro 2016")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Euro_2016_stade_de_France_France-Roumanie_%2827307532960%29.jpg/330px-Euro_2016_stade_de_France_France-Roumanie_%2827307532960%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Euro_2016_stade_de_France_France-Roumanie_(27307532960).jpg)

[France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team")'s 2–1 win over Romania at the [Stade de France](/wiki/Stade_de_France "Stade de France") opened the [UEFA Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016").

For the qualifying stage of the [Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying") Romania was drawn into [Group F](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying_Group_F "UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying Group F") along with Greece, Hungary, Finland, [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") and the Faroe Islands. Romania began its first successful qualification campaign since 2008 with a win over Greece before following up with a 1–1 draw with Pot 2 team Hungary and a 2–0 win over Finland. Despite the initial success, Romania decided to part with coach Victor Pițurcă by mutual consent. Anghel Iordănescu came out of retirement to return to coach Romania for a third time.

Under Iordănescu, Romania was able to follow up with comfortable 2–0 win over Northern Ireland and, despite a disappointing 1–0 win over the Faroe Islands and a 0–0 draw in the return game against Northern Ireland, Romania remained on top of Group F, one point above Northern Ireland and three points above third-placed Hungary. After a goalless draw in the match against Hungary in [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"), however, the team fell back on the second place, one point behind Northern Ireland and three above Hungary, still placed third. Following a 1–1 draw clinched in overtime at home against Finland, Romania secured their spot at the final tournament in the last game after a confident 3–0 win in the Faroe Islands. Romania finished the qualification group second, one point behind group winners Northern Ireland, completing their first successful qualification campaign in eight years undefeated after five wins and five draws. With only two goals conceded, Romania had the best defence in the qualifiers.

Romania advanced to [Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016"), where they were drawn in [Group A](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_Group_A "UEFA Euro 2016 Group A"), being named to play the opening match against the hosts [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"). The match began better for the Romanian side, who almost scored the first goal of the tournament in the fourth minute, after [Bogdan Stancu](/wiki/Bogdan_Stancu "Bogdan Stancu") tricked the French defence at a corner kick executed by his co-national [Nicușor Stanciu](/wiki/Nicolae_Stanciu_(footballer,_born_1993) "Nicolae Stanciu (footballer, born 1993)") and his shot was narrowly saved by the French goalkeeper [Hugo Lloris](/wiki/Hugo_Lloris "Hugo Lloris"). Shortly after the half-time, France dominated, and scored the first goal of the tournament after a header by [Olivier Giroud](/wiki/Olivier_Giroud "Olivier Giroud") in the 57th minute. Less than eight minutes later, Nicușor Stanciu was fouled by [Patrice Evra](/wiki/Patrice_Evra "Patrice Evra") in the French box, and Hungarian referee [Viktor Kassai](/wiki/Viktor_Kassai "Viktor Kassai") gave Romania a penalty which Bogdan Stancu scored. With the match coming to an end, just after Romania narrowly missed an opportunity after a free kick, [Dimitri Payet](/wiki/Dimitri_Payet "Dimitri Payet") shot hard from outside the box and scored France's second goal, crushing Romania's dream of a perfect start in the Euros.

In the second match, Romania faced [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team"), in a match that began with the Swiss side dominating. In the 17th minute, [Alexandru Chipciu](/wiki/Alexandru_Chipciu "Alexandru Chipciu") was fouled in the box, the second penalty of the tournament being accorded again to Romania. The same Bogdan Stancu went on and scored, giving an advantage for the Romanian side. Just after Switzerland almost scored an own goal, [Admir Mehmedi](/wiki/Admir_Mehmedi "Admir Mehmedi") scored for an equalizer in the 57th minute. The match eventually finished in a 1–1 draw.

With one point accumulated and on the third place in the group before the final match, Romania needed a victory against [Albania](/wiki/Albania_national_football_team "Albania national football team") in order to be among the first four best-third-placed teams and to qualify further in Euro. The match began good for the Romanian side, but [Armando Sadiku](/wiki/Armando_Sadiku "Armando Sadiku")'s header in the 43rd minute went past [Ciprian Tătărușanu](/wiki/Ciprian_T%C4%83t%C4%83ru%C8%99anu "Ciprian Tătărușanu"), giving Albania the lead and their first ever goal in a tournament. The despondent Romanian side failed to score in this match, with [Florin Andone](/wiki/Florin_Andone "Florin Andone") striking the post in the 76th minute. The negative score meant that Romania ended on the last place of the group, ending their Euro dream with no victory and after one draw, two defeats, two goals scored (both from penalties) and four conceded, with only one point, the poor results making the manager [Anghel Iordănescu](/wiki/Anghel_Iord%C4%83nescu "Anghel Iordănescu") to resign before the matches for the [2018 FIFA World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification") began three months later.

#### 2018 FIFA World Cup qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 2018 FIFA World Cup qualification")]

It was German manager [Christoph Daum](/wiki/Christoph_Daum "Christoph Daum") who took the place of Anghel Iordănescu for the next qualifying campaign. Due to FIFA's ranking revamp, Romania was drawn from Pot 1, having to face [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark_national_football_team "Denmark national football team"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team"), [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro_national_football_team "Montenegro national football team"), [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia_national_football_team "Armenia national football team") and [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan_national_football_team "Kazakhstan national football team"). The campaign began with a mediocre 1–1 draw against Montenegro in [Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca"), followed by a thrashing 5–0 victory in [Yerevan](/wiki/Yerevan "Yerevan") against Armenia before another mediocre draw in [Astana](/wiki/Astana "Astana") against Kazakhstan, a 0–3 defeat in Bucharest and a 1–3 defeat in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") against Poland, a lacklustre 0–0 draw against Denmark in Cluj-Napoca and a lucky victory in the last minutes against Armenia in Bucharest.

With only nine points after seven fixtures, Romania had to win against the revelation of the group, Montenegro, in [Podgorica](/wiki/Podgorica "Podgorica"), but failed amid a [Stevan Jovetić](/wiki/Stevan_Joveti%C4%87 "Stevan Jovetić") winner and were mathematically eliminated, leading to the sacking of Daum and his replacement with [Cosmin Contra](/wiki/Cosmin_Contra "Cosmin Contra"). This defeat was followed by a 3–1 victory against Kazakhstan in [Ploiești](/wiki/Ploie%C8%99ti "Ploiești") before a lucky 1–1 draw against Denmark in [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"). Romania ended fourth in the group, with 13 points, twelve goals scored and ten conceded.

#### 2018–19 UEFA Nations League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 2018–19 UEFA Nations League")]

The poor performance in the qualifiers meant Romania would be in the League C of UEFA's first season of the new competition, the [Nations League](/wiki/UEFA_Nations_League "UEFA Nations League"). They were drawn against neighbours [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia_national_football_team "Serbia national football team"), Montenegro again and [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states") side [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_national_football_team "Lithuania national football team").

In the first match, Romania registered yet another mediocre result against Montenegro, in a 0–0 draw in Ploiești, before a double comeback in Serbia in a 2–2 draw and a late-winner in [Vilnius](/wiki/Vilnius "Vilnius") against Lithuania. This was followed by another 0–0 draw in Bucharest against Serbia, in which [Dušan Tadić](/wiki/Du%C5%A1an_Tadi%C4%87 "Dušan Tadić") missed a penalty and a comfortable 3–0 victory over Lithuania in Ploiești.

Overall results meant that it was possible for Romania to win the group shall it win in Podgorica against Montenegro and Serbia lose to Lithuania in [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade"). Despite Romania actually emerging victorious from the duel against the Montenegrins, Serbia comfortably won against Lithuania and won the group. However, following UEFA's revamp of the competition, Romania, as the second place, promoted too in [League B for the next edition](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_UEFA_Nations_League_B "2020–21 UEFA Nations League B").

#### UEFA Euro 2020 qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: UEFA Euro 2020 qualification")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/UEFA_EURO_qualifiers_Sweden_vs_Romaina_20190323_42.jpg/250px-UEFA_EURO_qualifiers_Sweden_vs_Romaina_20190323_42.jpg)](/wiki/File:UEFA_EURO_qualifiers_Sweden_vs_Romaina_20190323_42.jpg)

Romania playing [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team") at [Friends Arena](/wiki/Friends_Arena "Friends Arena"), March 2019

For the qualifiers of Euro 2020, Romania was drawn in a group including [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team"), and [Norway](/wiki/Norway_national_football_team "Norway national football team") alongside [Malta](/wiki/Malta_national_football_team "Malta national football team") and [the Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands_national_football_team "Faroe Islands national football team"). In the opening game, Romania suffered a 1–2 defeat to Sweden in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), followed by a comfortable 4–1 victory over the Faroe Islands in Cluj-Napoca and a 2–2 draw with Norway in [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"), with [Claudiu Keșerü](/wiki/Claudiu_Ke%C8%99er%C3%BC "Claudiu Keșerü") scoring in the last minute.

Eventually, Romania stayed on track by defeating Malta in a 4–0 victory in Valletta, but it was later followed by a defeat to Spain in Bucharest, with two very late misses from [George Pușcaș](/wiki/George_Pu%C8%99ca%C8%99 "George Pușcaș") and [Dragoș Grigore](/wiki/Drago%C8%99_Grigore "Dragoș Grigore"), and a poor 1–0 victory against Malta. This meant Romania had to win against Norway in October, but after a goal from [Alexandru Mitriță](/wiki/Alexandru_Mitri%C8%9B%C4%83 "Alexandru Mitriță") in the 62nd minute, [Alexander Sørloth](/wiki/Alexander_S%C3%B8rloth "Alexander Sørloth") scored in the last minute and the match in Bucharest ended 1–1. This had significantly reduced their chances of automatic qualification, as they had to meet the Swedish and Spanish sides for the two remaining competitive games.

A 0–2 defeat to Sweden in Bucharest ensured Romania's fail to finish on any of the automatic qualification places. In the last matchday, they were thrashed by Spain in Madrid in a 0–5 defeat, meaning they would end the campaign with seventeen goals scored but also fifteen conceded. Due to the revamp of the previous Nations League and poor performance of Greece, Romania managed to grab the last spot for UEFA Euro 2020 qualifying play-offs, and was drawn in Path A. Shall it had won it, Romania would have been drawn in Group C of the final tournament, but it did not, Iceland winning in [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík") and eliminating them. Their performance cost Cosmin Contra his coaching position, as he was changed with [Mirel Rădoi](/wiki/Mirel_R%C4%83doi "Mirel Rădoi") before the play-offs.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

#### 2020–21 UEFA Nations League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: 2020–21 UEFA Nations League")]

For the second Nations League edition, Romania was drawn against Austria, Norway and Northern Ireland. A very lacklustre 1–1 draw against Northern Ireland in the first matchday was followed by an impressing 3–2 victory over Austria in [Klagenfurt am Wörthersee](/wiki/Klagenfurt_am_W%C3%B6rthersee "Klagenfurt am Wörthersee"). This meant that, after the first two matchdays, Romania was on the first place and supposed to promote to League A, but a 0–4 defeat to Norway in Oslo and a 0–1 defeat to Austria on homesoil meant Romania would fall back on the third place before the last two matches.

For the home match against Norway, the Romanians were given a 3–0 victory from UEFA after the impossibility of Norwegians to travel to Bucharest, following [Omar Elabdellaoui](/wiki/Omar_Elabdellaoui "Omar Elabdellaoui")'s positive test for [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") and the restrictive measures imposed by the Norwegian Ministry of Health. Another 1–1 draw against Northern Ireland in Belfast meant Romania would end on the third position and remain in League B.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

#### 2022 FIFA World Cup qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: 2022 FIFA World Cup qualification")]

Following an acceptable Nations League campaign, Romania was drawn from Pot 2 in the [2022 FIFA World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)"), in a group with [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team"), Euro 2016 revelation [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland_national_football_team "Iceland national football team") who eliminated them from [UEFA Euro 2020 qualifying play-offs](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020_qualifying_play-offs "UEFA Euro 2020 qualifying play-offs") two months prior, future revelation [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia_national_football_team "North Macedonia national football team"), Armenia again and [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein_national_football_team "Liechtenstein national football team"). In their first match, Romania managed a hard victory over North Macedonia in Bucharest after two quick Macedonian goals and a late winner from [Ianis Hagi](/wiki/Ianis_Hagi "Ianis Hagi"). Three days later, Germany managed a victory over them on the same stadium despite a huge opportunity from [Nicolae Stanciu](/wiki/Nicolae_Stanciu_(footballer,_born_1993) "Nicolae Stanciu (footballer, born 1993)") in the last minutes. This was followed by a shameful 2–3 defeat to Armenia in Yerevan.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Poor performance in the June friendlies, combined with the results so far gave the feeling of another poor World Cup campaign, just like last time, before a shocking 2–0 victory over Iceland in [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík"), followed by a mediocre 2–0 victory over Liechtenstein and a 0–0 draw in [Skopje](/wiki/Skopje "Skopje") against North Macedonia. Initial group revelation Armenia fell behind in the following matches and it was Romania and North Macedonia who'd fight for the second place, but despite a heroic defeat to Germany, in which Romania held the lead for almost an hour, and a victory over Armenia in Bucharest, a 0–0 draw against Iceland meant that Romania would depend on Iceland to hold a draw against North Macedonia in the last matchday. North Macedonia won, however, and went over Romania by a single point, eliminating them from another campaign.

#### 2022–23 UEFA Nations League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: 2022–23 UEFA Nations League")]

Romania was drawn from Pot 3 in the 2022–23 UEFA Nations League season, in a group against [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina_national_football_team "Bosnia and Herzegovina national football team"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland_national_football_team "Finland national football team") and Montenegro. In a similar scenario like five years prior, Montenegro comfortably won against Romania 2–0 in Podgorica, which was then followed by another defeat, to Bosnia and Herzegovina in [Zenica](/wiki/Zenica "Zenica") and a lacklustre victory over Finland in Bucharest.

With little to nothing achieved so far, Romania was crushed by Montenegro in Bucharest in a 0–3 defeat, meaning that chances to retain the place in League B were dim. A 1–1 draw against Finland in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki") meant Romania was again at the expense of other team, this time Montenegro, who needed not to lose against Finland, which eventually happened despite Romania's comfortable victory over eventual group winners Bosnia and Herzegovina. Therefore, Romania relegated to [League C for the next Nations League edition](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_UEFA_Nations_League_C "2024–25 UEFA Nations League C").

#### UEFA Euro 2024

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: UEFA Euro 2024")]

The Nations League performance meant Romania would be drawn from Pot 3 in Euro 2024 qualifiers, which led them to a [group](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_qualifying_Group_I "UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying Group I") with [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team"), [Israel](/wiki/Israel_national_football_team "Israel national football team"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_national_football_team "Kosovo national football team"), [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus_national_football_team "Belarus national football team") and [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra_national_football_team "Andorra national football team"). Two initial victories against Andorra and Belarus in Bucharest, followed by a lacklustre 0–0 against Kosovo in [Pristina](/wiki/Pristina "Pristina") gave the impression of another despondent campaign before a shocking comeback against Switzerland in [Lucerne](/wiki/Lucerne "Lucerne"), [Valentin Mihăilă](/wiki/Valentin_Mih%C4%83il%C4%83 "Valentin Mihăilă") scoring a brace in the last three minutes. This was then followed by a 1–1 draw against Israel in Bucharest before a [controversial](/wiki/Romania%E2%80%93Serbia_relations#Romania-Kosovo_relations "Romania–Serbia relations") match against Kosovo; the match was interrupted for almost an hour after Romanian fans began chastising Kosovar players and chanting [anti-NATO chants](/wiki/Serbophilia "Serbophilia"), with the Kosovars leaving the pitch in protest. Two late goals for Romania after the match restarted, a draw against Belarus on [neutral pitch](/wiki/Szusza_Ferenc_Stadion "Szusza Ferenc Stadion") because of their [involvement](/wiki/Belarusian_involvement_in_the_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Belarusian involvement in the Russian invasion of Ukraine") with the [Russian military operations in Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine") and a comfortable victory over Andorra meant Romania had to obtain a single point in the ninth matchday in order to mathematically qualify for Euro 2024.

In the ninth matchday. Romania faced a tired Israeli side, who had to play four games in nine days because of the Israel-Palestine war, [on neutral pitch](/wiki/Pancho_Ar%C3%A9na "Pancho Aréna"). [Eran Zahavi](/wiki/Eran_Zahavi "Eran Zahavi") stunned Romania in the 88th second of the match before [George Pușcaș](/wiki/George_Pu%C8%99ca%C8%99 "George Pușcaș") scored an equalizer eight minutes later. One hour in the match, Ianis Hagi assured Romania's qualification to Euro 2024 with a goal that meant Romania would win 2–1 over Israel. Moreover, Romania went on and won against Switzerland in Bucharest too, meaning they would win the group and be drawn from Pot 2 at the final tournament, in a heroic act of the Tricolours.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Romania_vs_Ukraine_EURO_2024.jpg/250px-Romania_vs_Ukraine_EURO_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Romania_vs_Ukraine_EURO_2024.jpg)

EURO 2024 Matchday 1 game opening Romania vs Ukraine

At the final tournament, Romania was drawn against [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team"), [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_national_football_team "Slovakia national football team") and neighbours [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine_national_football_team "Ukraine national football team") in [Group E](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_Group_E "UEFA Euro 2024 Group E"). In the first matchday, Romania met Ukraine in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), and achieved their best result in the [European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") final tournament: a Nicolae Stanciu screamer that came in as the fourth most beautiful goal of the tournament, a long shot from [Răzvan Marin](/wiki/R%C4%83zvan_Marin "Răzvan Marin") and a tap-in from [Denis Drăguș](/wiki/Denis_Dr%C4%83gu%C8%99 "Denis Drăguș") gave Romania a 3–0 victory after not playing in an international tournament for eight years. The victory was only the second victory of Romania at a European Championship, the first and only other one being against [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") in [2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000") in a 3–2 with a last minute winner.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Following this performance, in the second matchday, Romania was stunned by [Youri Tielemans](/wiki/Youri_Tielemans "Youri Tielemans") of Belgium in the 74th second, and despite putting a decent fight, lost the match 0–2, [Kevin De Bruyne](/wiki/Kevin_De_Bruyne "Kevin De Bruyne") scoring a controversial goal in the final minutes.

All the four teams had the same amount of points at the end of the second matchday, three, but because of the overall goal difference, Romania was on the first position at this point, with three goals scored and a +1 difference. In the third matchday, Slovakia and Romania drew in a comfortable match as both teams assured qualification at that score, as neither Ukraine or Belgium managed to win the other match. With the table unchanged from the second matchday, Romania was crowned as the group winner despite all the teams ending with four points, having a victory against fourth place Ukraine, a better goal difference than third place Slovakia and more goals scored than second place Belgium.

In the Round of 16, Romania returned to Munich and faced [the Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team"). Despite a good start, [Cody Gakpo](/wiki/Cody_Gakpo "Cody Gakpo") stunned the Tricolours in the 20th minute, before [Donyell Malen](/wiki/Donyell_Malen "Donyell Malen")'s brace in the last minutes. Despite losing 0–3 against the Dutch side and leaving the tournament relatively early, Romania's performance was praised by numerous figures of the football world, achieving a very good result for the overall level of the national team.

Romania then secured promotion to [League B](/wiki/2026%E2%80%9327_UEFA_Nations_League_B "2026–27 UEFA Nations League B") for the [2026-2027 edition of the Nations League](/wiki/2026%E2%80%9327_UEFA_Nations_League "2026–27 UEFA Nations League") thanks to a perfect record of 6 wins in as many matches against their 3 opponents, [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_national_football_team "Kosovo national football team"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus_national_football_team "Cyprus national football team") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_national_football_team "Lithuania national football team"), including a green-card home win in the return against Kosovo after the Kosovar players decided to abandon the match following incidents in the stands.

However, Romania fell back into the pattern of recent years and jeopardised their chances of qualification by losing at home at the start of the [2026 World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_H "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group H") against [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina_national_football_team "Bosnia and Herzegovina national football team") (0–1), their main rival for the 2nd play-off place behind group favourites [Austria](/wiki/Austria_national_football_team "Austria national football team"), on 21 March 2025, while the Bosnians were still on a negative run; after a disappointing Romanian performance against an opponent that was more aggressive both offensively and defensively, and during a match that saw a refereeing controversy with a penalty not awarded to the Romanians for a Bosnian handball in the penalty area.
