#### Hungary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Hungary")]

Romania's most fierce rivalry is against their neighbours [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_national_football_team "Hungary national football team"). The reason for the rivalry are the general feelings for the other country, as Hungary and Romania have a [long-standing dispute](/wiki/Hungary%E2%80%93Romania_relations#Conflict "Hungary–Romania relations") over certain parts of Romania that were historically part of Hungary, most well-known being [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"). This has been the subject of the constant squabble between Hungarian and Romanian fans during the direct matches of the two countries or clubs from them.

The first match between the two countries took place in Bucharest, on 4 October 1936. Romania initially took the lead through [Silviu Bindea](/wiki/Silviu_Bindea "Silviu Bindea"), but two goals in the second half scored by [Gyula Lázár](/wiki/Gyula_L%C3%A1z%C3%A1r "Gyula Lázár") and [Géza Toldi](/wiki/G%C3%A9za_Toldi "Géza Toldi") led to Hungary's victory. At that moment, Hungary was, if not the best, among the best teams on the planet, and after this result, impressive results of the Hungarians were obtained during their matches against Romania, most notably the worst defeat of Romania to date, a 0–9 defeat in Budapest on 6 June 1948. Other notable Hungarian victories include the 7–2 on 30 September 1945, two 5-1s, one in Bucharest on 24 October 1948 and one in Budapest on 19 September 1954, and a 3–0 in Bucharest on 12 October 1947.

Romania's first victory against Hungary came almost 63 years after the first match, in the seventeenth derby overall. Romania won 2–0 in Bucharest on 5 June 1999, in the [qualifying stage of UEFA Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000_qualifying_Group_7 "UEFA Euro 2000 qualifying Group 7"). Since then, Hungary has yet to defeat Romania again, and Romania won five more times, including a notable 3–0 in the [qualifying stage of the 2014 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_D "2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group D").

Due to the general antipathy between the two peoples, derbies often led to skirmishes between the two fans. The worst incidents were recorded on 6 September 2013 and 11 October 2014, during the derbies of the two countries in Bucharest for the 2014 FIFA World Cup and [UEFA Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying_Group_F "UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying Group F"), respectively. During the first match, Hungarians lit many flames, booed the [Romanian anthem](/wiki/De%C8%99teapt%C4%83-te,_rom%C3%A2ne! "Deșteaptă-te, române!"), and after the 0–3 defeat were quickly transported back home, not before some of them fought with Romanian fans in the [Old Town of Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest_Old_Town "Bucharest Old Town"). After Romania's elimination in the [Second Round](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_second_round "2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round") by [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team"), many Hungarians thanked Greeks and publicly insulted Romania on their social media accounts. One year later, the fights between the two groups of fans led to 58 people seeking medical care after Romanian fans jumped the fence separating their sector from the one Hungarian fans were in and attacked them. The match ended 1-1 and fights were again commenced in the Old Town, where many terraces of the local coffee shops and bars were destroyed by Hungarian fans.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Notable opportunities when the two teams have met include: [the football tournament of the 1952 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_1952_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1952 Summer Olympics"), the qualifying stages for UEFA Euro [1972](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1972_quarter-finals "UEFA Euro 1972 quarter-finals"), [2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2000 qualifying") and 2016 and the qualifying stages for the FIFA World Cups of [1982](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_4 "1982 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 4"), [2002](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_8 "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 8") and 2014.

#### Greece

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Greece")]

A notable rivalry was built with Greece, as it is the team Romania has met the most times: thirty-six (Romania met Yugoslavia thirty-seven times, a record which was annulled after the dismantle of Yugoslavia). Out of all the matches overall, Romania won eighteen of them, dominating the rivalry. Greece won an additional eight matches and ten ended in a draw.

The only notable opportunity the two teams have met was in the Second Round of the 2014 FIFA World Cup, to which Greece qualifying after defeating Romania in a two-legged play-off, winning 3–1 in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") and drawing 1–1 in Bucharest.

#### Minor rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Minor rivalries")]

##### Montenegro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Montenegro")]

In the last period, a small rivalry was built against [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro_national_football_team "Montenegro national football team"). The two teams met a total of seven times, and Romania's 4–0 victory in a friendly on 31 May 2008 was Montenegro's biggest defeat up to a 0–7 defeat in London in 2019. The teams met in the qualifying stage of the [2018 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_E "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group E"), and it was Montenegro who eliminated Romania after a 1–0 victory in [Podgorica](/wiki/Podgorica "Podgorica") which came after a 1–1 draw on Romanian soil. The two teams met again one year later, in [Group 4 of League C](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_UEFA_Nations_League_C#Group_4 "2018–19 UEFA Nations League C") of the [inaugural season](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_UEFA_Nations_League "2018–19 UEFA Nations League") of the [UEFA Nations League](/wiki/UEFA_Nations_League "UEFA Nations League"). Romania avenged itself with a 1–0 victory in Podgorica, which was not enough however to win the group over mutual neighbour [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia_national_football_team "Serbia national football team"). In the third edition of the same competition, the two teams met again, this time Montenegro decimating Romania twice, 2–0 in Podgorica and 3–0 in Bucharest.

##### Kosovo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Kosovo")]

Romania is a strong supporter of Serbia's territorial integrity and [has not recognized the legitimacy](/wiki/International_recognition_of_Kosovo "International recognition of Kosovo") of [Kosovo's independence from Serbia](/wiki/2008_Kosovo_declaration_of_independence "2008 Kosovo declaration of independence") on 17 February 2008. The two teams met only four times, but all the matches led to problems and antipathy feelings between the two peoples.

###### 2023 match

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: 2023 match")]

On 12 September 2023, the two teams met in Bucharest for the [qualifying stage of UEFA Euro 2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_qualifying_Group_I "UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying Group I"). In the eighteenth minute, the Kosovar players left the pitch in a protest after a bottle was thrown from the Romanian sector, which was combined with the two banners quoting "Basarabia e România” (”[Bessarabia is Romania](/wiki/Bessarabia,_Romanian_land "Bessarabia, Romanian land")” in [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language")) and ”Kosovo je Srbija” (”[Kosovo is Serbia](/wiki/Kosovo_is_Serbia "Kosovo is Serbia")” in [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language")) and the constant [pro-Serbia chants](/wiki/Serbophilia "Serbophilia") of the Romanian extremist fanbase ”Uniți sub Tricolor” (”United under Tricolour”). The match was interrupted for fifty minutes and the [Football Federation of Kosovo](/wiki/Football_Federation_of_Kosovo "Football Federation of Kosovo") appealed for a 3–0 victory over Romania because of the incidents, which was turned down by UEFA. Romania won the match 2-0.

###### 2024 matches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: 2024 matches")]

The two teams have met again in the first leg match in [group of League C](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_UEFA_Nations_League_C#Group_2 "2024–25 UEFA Nations League C") of the UEFA Nations League, season [2024–25](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_UEFA_Nations_League "2024–25 UEFA Nations League"). The match took place on Kosovo's [Fadil Vokrri Stadium](/wiki/Fadil_Vokrri_Stadium "Fadil Vokrri Stadium"), on 6 September . Once again, the mutual animosity between the two sides was evident, as during the opening ceremony, the Kosovar fans booed while the Romanian anthem was playing. At the end of the match, after a decisive 3–0 victory for Romania, the Kosovar fans threw various objects at Romania's squad while they were leaving the pitch.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

When Romania played against [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_national_football_team "Lithuania national football team") on 9 September, Romanian fans chanted anti-Kosovo messages which resulted in a €52,500 fine and a partial closure of [Arena Națională](/wiki/Arena_Na%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Arena Națională") for the match against Kosovo the very next month.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Already a tense match after the incidents in Kosovo and Lithuania, the two sides faced each other again in Bucharest. A victory for the hosts would have meant promotion to League B of the [competition's next season](/wiki/2026%E2%80%9327_UEFA_Nations_League "2026–27 UEFA Nations League"). Kosovo needed a 3 goals difference victory in order to take Romania's spot before the last matchday, and dominated the match. As the final whistle was approaching, after suffering a tackle, Kosovar player [Albion Rrahmani](/wiki/Albion_Rrahmani "Albion Rrahmani") attacked Romanian player [Denis Alibec](/wiki/Denis_Alibec "Denis Alibec"), before both teams began fighting on the pitch, the Danish referee booking three players, two of Kosovo and one of Romania. The Romanians proceeded to take a free kick, a moment again disrupted by the Kosovars as they walked over the Romanian fan sector to engage in a verbal fight with the Romanian fans who allegedly began shouting "Serbia! Serbia!".[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In the 97th minute of the match, four minutes after the fight between Alibec and Rrahamni took place, the Kosovars left the pitch despite [their manager](/wiki/Franco_Foda "Franco Foda")'s plead not to. While leaving the pitch, Kosovo's goalkeeper [Arijanet Muric](/wiki/Arijanet_Muric "Arijanet Muric") was seen showing the middle finger to the Romanian fans. The Romanian team remained on the pitch for one more hour until the referee suspended the match. Meanwhile, the Kosovar players trashed their changing room, leaked pictures of trash and pizza being posted online. Problems continued even after this, with both delegations almost fighting again in the press conference room.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The match was counted as "abandoned". No matter the final result, Romania was crowned as the group winner after a 4–1 victory against [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus_national_football_team "Cyprus national football team") three days later, despite the final result still pending. However, on 20 November, five days after the match, UEFA announced that Romania won the match 3–0, but will have to play one match at home [without fans](/wiki/Behind_closed_doors_(sport) "Behind closed doors (sport)") and face a €128,000 fine, while Kosovo would also receive a €6,000 fine for forfeiting the match.

### Kits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Kits")]

Romania's kits have been supplied by Spanish company [Joma](/wiki/Joma "Joma") from 2015, which replaced [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") following a three-decade contract[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. In 2017, the Romanian Football Federation announced its first brand identity and a new kit; the new emblem references the coat of arms of all five Romanian provinces with the intention to symbolise the unity of Romania.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In March 2025, Romanian Football Federation launched a new home and away kit for the 2025–26 World Cup Qualifiers, designed in collaboration with Jean Octavian Popescu.[source](https://www.frf.ro/nationale/masculin/echipa-nationala/fotovideo-federatia-romana-de-fotbal-lanseaza-noul-echipament-al-echipei-nationale-tricoul-care-ne-face-vazuti/)

### Home stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Home stadium")]

The Romania national team mainly plays its home games at the [Arena Națională](/wiki/Arena_Na%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Arena Națională") in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"), the largest stadium in the country. It opened in 2011 and has a capacity of 55,600 seats. The stadium is a [Category 4](/wiki/UEFA_stadium_categories "UEFA stadium categories") venue and hosted the [2012 Europa League final](/wiki/2012_UEFA_Europa_League_Final "2012 UEFA Europa League Final") and [Euro 2020](/wiki/Euro_2020 "Euro 2020").

Other games, including [friendlies](/wiki/Exhibition_game#Association_football "Exhibition game") and World Cup and Euro qualifiers, have been played at other venues such as the [Steaua Stadium](/wiki/Stadionul_Steaua_(2021) "Stadionul Steaua (2021)") (Bucharest), the [Ion Oblemenco Stadium](/wiki/Stadionul_Ion_Oblemenco "Stadionul Ion Oblemenco") ([Craiova](/wiki/Craiova "Craiova")), the [Cluj Arena](/wiki/Cluj_Arena "Cluj Arena") ([Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca")), the smaller [Dr. Constantin Rădulescu](/wiki/Stadionul_Dr._Constantin_R%C4%83dulescu "Stadionul Dr. Constantin Rădulescu") (Cluj-Napoca), [Ilie Oană](/wiki/Ilie_Oan%C4%83_Stadium "Ilie Oană Stadium") ([Ploiești](/wiki/Ploie%C8%99ti "Ploiești")) and [Rapid-Giulești](/wiki/Stadionul_Rapid-Giule%C8%99ti "Stadionul Rapid-Giulești") (Bucharest) stadiums.

Romania's [UEFA Nations League](/wiki/UEFA_Nations_League "UEFA Nations League") games, major tournament qualifiers and friendlies are to be televised on [Pro TV](/wiki/Pro_TV "Pro TV") up until 2022. Between 2008 and 2014, [Antena 1](/wiki/Antena_1_(Romania) "Antena 1 (Romania)") had the rights to broadcast the country's home matches, friendlies and qualifiers. From 2014 to 2018, Romania's qualifying matches for the European Championship and the World Cup, plus two pre-Euro and one post-Euro friendly match were taken over by [TVR](/wiki/Romanian_Television "Romanian Television"). The friendly matches that were not broadcast by TVR were taken over by Pro TV. In March 2019, the latter took over all broadcasts of Romania's fixtures from TVR, with the effective broadcasting starting in September 2018.

### Summer Olympics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Summer Olympics")]

[Football at the Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Football at the Summer Olympics") was first played officially in [1908](/wiki/Football_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1908 Summer Olympics"). The Olympiads between 1896 and 1980 were only open for [amateur](/wiki/Amateur_football "Amateur football") players. The 1984 and 1988 tournaments were open to players with no appearances in the World Cup. After the 1988 Olympics, the football event was changed into a tournament for U23 teams, with a maximum of three older players. See [Romania Olympic football team](/wiki/Romania_Olympic_football_team "Romania Olympic football team") for competition records from 1992 until present day.
